column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=From the causes of neglect described, Newera Ellia was in the deserted\r
#Text=and wretched state in which I saw it; but so infatuated was I in the\r
#Text=belief that its importance must be appreciated when the knowledge of\r
#Text=its climate was more widely extended that I looked forward to its\r
#Text=becoming at some future time a rival to the Neilgherries station in\r
#Text=India.
#Text=My ideas were based upon the natural features of the place,\r
#Text=combined with its requirements.
#Text=It apparently produced nothing except potatoes.
#Text=The soil was supposed\r
#Text=to be as good as it appeared to be.
#Text=The quality of the water and the\r
#Text=supply were unquestionable; the climate could not be surpassed for\r
#Text=salubrity.
#Text=There was a carriage road from Colombo, one hundred and\r
#Text=fifteen miles, and from Kandy, forty-seven miles; the last thirteen\r
#Text=being the Rambodde Pass, arriving at an elevation of six thousand six\r
#Text=hundred feet, from which point a descent of two miles terminated the\r
#Text=road to Newera Ellia.
#Text=The great proof of the general poverty of Ceylon is shown in the\r
#Text=failure of every agricultural experiment in which a rich soil is\r
#Text=required.
#Text=Cinnamon thrives; but why?
#Text=It delights in a soil of quartz sand, in\r
#Text=which nothing else would grow.
#Text=Cocoa-nut trees flourish for the same reason; sea air, a sandy soil and\r
#Text=a dry subsoil are all that the cocoa-nut requires.
#Text=On the other hand, those tropical productions which require a strong\r
#Text=soil invariably prove failures, and sugar, cotton, indigo, hemp and\r
#Text=tobacco cannot possibly be cultivated with success.
#Text=Any person who thoroughly understands the practical cultivation of the\r
#Text=sugar-cane can tell the quality of sugar that will be produced by an\r
#Text=examination of the soil.
#Text=I am thoroughly convinced that no soil in\r
#Text=Ceylon will produce a sample of fine, straw-colored, dry, bright,\r
#Text=large-crystaled sugar.
#Text=The finest sample ever produced of Ceylon sugar\r
#Text=is a dull gray, and always moist, requiring a very large proportion of\r
#Text=lime in the manufacture, without which it could neither be cleansed nor\r
#Text=crystalized.
#Text=The sugar cane, to produce fine sugar, requires a rich, stiff, and very\r
#Text=dry soil.
#Text=In Ceylon, there is no such thing as a stiff soil existing.
#Text=The alluvial soil upon the banks of rivers is adapted for the growth of\r
#Text=cotton and tobacco, but not for the sugar-cane.
#Text=In such light and\r
#Text=moist alluvial soil the latter will grow to a great size, and will\r
#Text=yield a large quantity of juice in which the saccharometer may stand\r
#Text=well; but the degree of strength indicated will proceed from an immense\r
#Text=proportion of mucilage, which will give much trouble in the cleansing\r
#Text=during boiling; and the sugar produced must be wanting in dryness and\r
#Text=fine color.
#Text=The chief article of native consumption, "rice," should be an export\r
#Text=from Ceylon; but there has been an unaccountable neglect on the part of\r
#Text=government regarding the production of this important grain, for the\r
#Text=supply of which Ceylon is mainly dependent upon importation.
#Text=In the\r
#Text=hitherto overrated general resources of Ceylon, the cultivation of rice\r
#Text=has scarcely been deemed worthy of notice; the all-absorbing subject of\r
#Text=coffee cultivation has withdrawn the attention of the government from\r
#Text=that particular article, for the production of which the resources of\r
#Text=Ceylon are both naturally and artificially immense.
#Text=This neglect is the more extraordinary as the increase of coffee\r
#Text=cultivation involves a proportionate increase in the consumption of\r
#Text=rice, by the additional influx of coolie labor from the coast of India;\r
#Text=therefore the price and supply of rice in Ceylon become questions of\r
#Text=similar importance to the price of corn in England.
#Text=This dependence\r
#Text=upon a foreign soil for the supply involves the necessary fluctuations\r
#Text=in price caused by uncertain arrivals and precarious harvests; and the\r
#Text=importance of an unlimited supply at an even rate may be imagined when\r
#Text=it is known that every native consumes a bushel of rice per month, when\r
#Text=he can obtain it.
#Text=How little can the inhabitant of a cold or temperate climate appreciate\r
#Text=the vast amount of "life" in a tropical country.
#Text=The combined action\r
#Text=of light, heat and moisture calls into existence myriads of creeping\r
#Text=things, the offspring of the decay of vegetation.
#Text="Life" appears to\r
#Text=emanate from "death"--the destruction of one material seems to multify\r
#Text=the existence of another--the whole surface of the earth seems busied\r
#Text=in one vast system of giving birth.
#Text=Ceylon does not descend to second-class diseases: there is no such\r
#Text=thing as influenza; whooping-cough, measles, scarlatina, etc., are\r
#Text=rarely, if ever, heard of; we ring the changes upon four first-class\r
#Text=ailments--four scourges, which alternately ascend to the throne of\r
#Text=pestilence and annually reduce the circle of our friends--cholera,\r
#Text=dysentery, small-pox and fever.
#Text=This year (1854) there has been some\r
#Text=dispute as to the routine of succession; they have accordingly all\r
#Text=raged at one time.
#Text=The cause of infection in disease has long been a subject of\r
#Text=controversy among medical men, but there can be little doubt that,\r
#Text=whatever is the origin of the disease, the same is the element of\r
#Text=infection.
#Text=The question is, therefore, reduced to the prime cause of\r
#Text=the disease itself.
#Text=A theory that animalcules are the cause of the various contagious and\r
#Text=infectious disorders has created much discussion; and although this\r
#Text=opinion is not generally entertained by the faculty, the idea is so\r
#Text=feasible, and so many rational arguments can be brought forward in its\r
#Text=support, that I cannot help touching upon a topic so generally\r
#Text=interesting.
#Text=In the first place, nearly all infectious diseases predominate in\r
#Text=localities which are hot, damp, swampy, abounding in stagnant pools and\r
#Text=excluded from a free circulation of air.
#Text=In a tropical country, a\r
#Text=residence in such a situation would be certain death to a human being,\r
#Text=but the same locality will be found to swarm with insects and reptiles\r
#Text=of all classes.
#Text=Thus, what is inimical to human life is propitious to the insect tribe.
#Text=This is the first step in favor of the argument.
#Text=Therefore, whatever\r
#Text=shall tend to increase the insect life must in an inverse ratio war\r
#Text=with human existence.
#Text=When we examine a drop of impure water, and discover by the microscope\r
#Text=the thousands of living beings which not only are invisible to the\r
#Text=naked eye, but some of whom are barely discoverable even by the\r
#Text=strongest magnifying power, it certainly leads to the inference, that\r
#Text=if one drop of impure fluid contains countless atoms endowed with\r
#Text=vitality, the same amount of impure air may be equally tenanted with\r
#Text=its myriads of invisible inhabitants.
#Text=It is well known that different mixtures, which are at first pure and\r
#Text=apparently free from all insect life, will, in the course of their\r
#Text=fermentation and subsequent impurity, generate peculiar species of\r
#Text=animalcules.
#Text=Thus all water and vegetable or animal matter, in a state\r
#Text=of stagnation and decay, gives birth to insect life; likewise all\r
#Text=substances of every denomination which are subjected to putrid\r
#Text=fermentation.
#Text=Unclean sewers, filthy hovels, unswept streets, unwashed\r
#Text=clothes, are therefore breeders of animalcules, many of which are\r
#Text=perfectly visible without microscopic aid.
#Text=Now, if some are discernible by the naked eye, and others are detected\r
#Text=in such varying sizes that some can only just be distinguished by the\r
#Text=most powerful lens, is it not rational to conclude that the smallest\r
#Text=discernible to human intelligence is but the medium of a countless\r
#Text=race?
#Text=that millions of others still exist, which are too minute for any\r
#Text=observation?
#Text=Observe the particular quarters of a city which suffers most severely\r
#Text=during the prevalence of an epidemic, In all dirty, narrow streets,\r
#Text=where the inhabitants are naturally of a low and uncleanly class, the\r
#Text=cases will be tenfold.
#Text=Thus, filth is admitted to have at least the\r
#Text=power of attracting disease, and we know that it not only attracts, but\r
#Text=generates animalcules; therefore filth, insects and disease are ever to\r
#Text=be seen closely linked together.
#Text=Now, the common preventives against infection are such as are\r
#Text=peculiarly inimical to every kind of insect; camphor, chloride of lime,\r
#Text=tobacco-smoke, and powerful scents and smokes of any kind.
#Text=The first\r
#Text=impulse on the appearance of an infectious disease is to purify\r
#Text=everything as much as possible, and by extra cleanliness and\r
#Text=fumigations to endeavor to arrest its progress.
#Text=The great purifier of\r
#Text=Nature is a violent wind, which usually terminates an epidemic\r
#Text=immediately; this would naturally carry before it all insect life with\r
#Text=which the atmosphere might be impregnated, and the disease disappears\r
#Text=at the same moment.
#Text=It will he well remembered that the plague of\r
#Text=locusts inflicted upon Pharaoh was relieved in the same manner: "And\r
#Text=the Lord turned a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts\r
#Text=and cast them into the Red Sea; there remained not one locust in all\r
#Text=the coasts of Egypt."
#Text=Every person is aware that unwholesome air is quite poisonous to the\r
#Text=human system as impure water; and seeing that the noxious qualities of\r
#Text=the latter are caused by animalcules, and that the method used for\r
#Text=purifying infected air are those most generally destructive to insect\r
#Text=life, it is not irrational to conclude that the poisonous qualities of\r
#Text=bad water and bad air arise from the same cause.
#Text=Man is being constantly preyed upon by insects; and were it not for\r
#Text=ordinary cleanliness, he would become a mass of vermin; even this does\r
#Text=not protect him from the rapacity of ticks, mosquitoes, fleas and many\r
#Text=others.
#Text=Intestinal worms feed on him within, and, unseen, use their\r
#Text=slow efforts for his destruction.
#Text=The knowledge of so many classes which actually prey upon the human\r
#Text=system naturally leads to the belief that many others endowed with the\r
#Text=same propensities exist, of which we have at present no conception.
#Text=Thus, different infectious disorders might proceed from peculiar\r
#Text=species of animalcules, which, at given periods, are wafted into\r
#Text=certain countries, carrying pestilence and death in their invisible\r
#Text=course.
#Text=A curious phenomenon has recently occurred at Mauritus, where that\r
#Text=terrible scourge, the cholera, has been raging with desolating effect.
#Text=There is a bird in that island called the "martin," but it is more\r
#Text=property the "mina."
#Text=This bird is about the size of the starling, whose\r
#Text=habits its possesses in a great degree.
#Text=It exists in immense numbers,\r
#Text=and is a grand destroyer of all insects.
#Text=On this account it is seldom\r
#Text=or never shot at, especially as it is a great comforter to all cattle,\r
#Text=whose hides it entirely cleans from ticks and other vermin, remaining\r
#Text=for many hours perched upon the back of one animal, while its bill is\r
#Text=actively employed in searching out and destroying every insect.
#Text=During the prevalence of the cholera at Mauritius these birds\r
#Text=disappeared.
#Text=Such a circumstance had never before occurred, and the\r
#Text=real cause of their departure is still a mystery.
#Text=May it not have been, that some species of insect upon which they fed\r
#Text=had likewise migrated, and that certain noxious animalcules, which had\r
#Text=been kept down by this class, had thus multiplied within the atmosphere\r
#Text=until their numbers caused disease?
#Text=All suppositions on such a subject\r
#Text=must, however, remain in obscurity, as no proof can be adduced of their\r
#Text=correctness.
#Text=The time may arrive when science may successfully grapple\r
#Text=with all human ailments, but hitherto that king of pestilence, the\r
#Text="cholera," has reduced the highest medical skill to miserable\r
#Text=uncertainty.
#Text=Upon reconsidering the dangers of fevers, dysentery, etc., in the\r
#Text=swampy and confined districts described, the naturalist may become\r
#Text=somewhat less ardent in following his favorite pursuit.
#Text=Of one fact I\r
#Text=can assure him that no matter how great the natural strength of his\r
#Text=constitution, the repeated exposure to the intense heat of the sun, the\r
#Text=unhealthy districts that he will visit, the nights redolent of malaria,\r
#Text=and the horrible water that he must occasionally drink, will gradually\r
#Text=undermine the power of the strongest man.
#Text=Both sportsman and\r
#Text=naturalist in this must share alike.
#Text=No one who has not actually suffered from the effect can appreciate the\r
#Text=misery of bad water in a tropical country, or the blessings of a cool,\r
#Text=pure draught.
#Text=I have been in districts of Ceylon where for sixteen or\r
#Text=twenty miles not a drop of water is to be obtained fit for an animal to\r
#Text=drink; not a tree to throw a few yards of shade upon the parching\r
#Text=ground; nothing but stunted, thorny jungles and sandy, barren plains as\r
#Text=far as the eye can reach; the yellow leaves crisp upon the withered\r
#Text=branches, the wild fruits hardened for want of sap, all moisture robbed\r
#Text=from vegetation by the pitiless drought of several months.
#Text=A day's work in such a country is hard indeed carrying a heavy rifle\r
#Text=for some five-and-twenty miles, sometimes in deep sand, sometimes on\r
#Text=good ground, but always exposed to the intensity of that blaze, added\r
#Text=to the reflection from the sandy soil, and the total want of fresh air\r
#Text=and water.
#Text=All Nature seems stagnated; a distant pool is seen, and a\r
#Text=general rush takes place toward the cheering sight.
#Text=The water is\r
#Text=thicker than pea soup, a green scum floats through the thickened mass,\r
#Text=and the temperature is upward of 130 Fahrenheit.
#Text=All kinds of insects\r
#Text=are swarming in the putrid fluid, and a saltish bitter adds to its\r
#Text=nauseating flavor.
#Text=I have seen the exhausted coolies spread their dirty\r
#Text=cloths on the surface, and form them into filters by sucking the water\r
#Text=through them.
#Text=Oh for a glass of Newera Ellia water, the purest and\r
#Text=best that ever flows, as it sparkles out of the rocks on the\r
#Text=mountain-tops!
#Text=what pleasure so perfect as a long, deep and undisturbed\r
#Text=draught of such cold, clear nectar when the throat is parched with\r
#Text=unquenchable thirst!
#Text=In some parts of Ceylon, especially in the neighborhood of the coast,\r
#Text=where the land is flat and sandy, the water is always brackish, even\r
#Text=during the rainy season, and in the dry months it is undrinkable.
#Text=The natives then make use of a berry for cleansing it and precipitating\r
#Text=the impurities.
#Text=II know the shrub and the berry well, but it has no\r
#Text=English denomination.
#Text=The berries are about the size of a very large\r
#Text=pea, and grow in clusters of from ten to fifteen together, and one\r
#Text=berry is said to be sufficient to cleanse a gallon of water.
#Text=The\r
#Text=method of using them is curious, although simple.
#Text=The vessel which is\r
#Text=intended to contain the water, which is generally an earthen chatty, is\r
#Text=well rubbed in the inside with a berry until the latter, which is of a\r
#Text=horny consistency, like vegetable ivory, is completely worn away.
#Text=The\r
#Text=chatty is then filled with the muddy water, and allowed to stand for\r
#Text=about an hour or more, until all the impurities have precipitated to\r
#Text=the bottom and the water remains clear.
#Text=I have constantly used this berry, but I certainly cannot say that the\r
#Text=water has ever been rendered perfectly clear; it has been vastly\r
#Text=improved, and what was totally undrinkable before has been rendered fit\r
#Text=for use; but it has at the best been only comparatively good; and\r
#Text=although the berry has produced a decided effect, the native accounts\r
#Text=of its properties are greatly exaggerated.
#Text=During the prolonged droughts, many rivers of considerable magnitude\r
#Text=are completely exhausted, and nothing remains but a dry bed of said\r
#Text=between lofty banks.
#Text=At these seasons the elephants, being hard\r
#Text=pressed for water, make use of their wonderful instinct by digging\r
#Text=holes in the dry sand of the river's bed; this they perform with the\r
#Text=horny toes of their fore feet, and frequently work to a depth of three\r
#Text=feet before they discover the liquid treasure beneath.
#Text=This process of\r
#Text=well-digging almost oversteps the boundaries of instinct and strongly,\r
#Text=savors of reason, the two powers being so nearly connected that it is\r
#Text=difficult in some cases to define the distinction.
#Text=There are so many\r
#Text=interesting cases of the wonderful display of both these attributes in\r
#Text=animals, that I shall notice some features of this subject in a\r
#Text=separate chapter.
#Text=Among the inexperienced there is a prevalent idea connected with\r
#Text=tropical forests and jungles that they teem with wild fruits, which\r
#Text=Nature is supposed to produce spontaneously.
#Text=Nothing can be more\r
#Text=erroneous than such an opinion; even edible berries are scantily\r
#Text=supplied by the wild shrubs and trees, and these, in lieu of others of\r
#Text=superior quality, are sometimes dignified by the name of fruit.
#Text=The guava and the katumbillé are certainly very numerous throughout the\r
#Text=Ouva district; the latter being a dark red, rough-skinned kind of plum,\r
#Text=the size of a greengage, but free from stone.
#Text=It grows upon a thorny\r
#Text=bush about fifteen feet high; but the fruit is too acid to please most\r
#Text=palates; the extreme thirst produced by a day's shooting in a burning\r
#Text=sun makes it refreshing when plucked from the tree; but it does not\r
#Text=aspire to the honor of a place at a table, where it can only appear in\r
#Text=the form of red currant jelly, for which it is an undeniable substitute.
#Text=Excellent blackberries and a very large and full-flavored black\r
#Text=raspberry grow at Newera Ellia; likewise the Cape gooseberry, which is\r
#Text=of the genus "solanum."
#Text=The latter is a round yellow berry, the size of\r
#Text=a cherry; this is enclosed in a loose bladder, which forms an outer\r
#Text=covering.
#Text=The flavor is highly aromatic, but, like most Ceylon wild\r
#Text=fruits, it is too acid.
#Text=The sweetest and the best of the jungle productions is the "morra."
#Text=This is a berry about the size of a small nutmeg, which grows in\r
#Text=clusters upon a large tree of rich dark foliage.
#Text=The exterior of the\r
#Text=berry is brown and slightly rough; the skin, or rather the case, is\r
#Text=brittle and of the consistence of an egg-shell; this, when broken and\r
#Text=peeled off, exposes a semi-transparent pulp, like a skinned grape in\r
#Text=appearance and in flavor.
#Text=It is extremely juicy but, unfortunately, a\r
#Text=large black stone occupies the centre and at least one-half of the bulk\r
#Text=of the entire fruit.
#Text=The jambo apple is a beautiful fruit in appearance being the facsimile\r
#Text=of a snow-white pear formed of wax, with a pink blush upon one side.
#Text=Its exterior beauty is all that it can boast of, as the fruit itself is\r
#Text=vapid and tasteless.
#Text=In fact, all wild fruits are, for the most part,\r
#Text=great exaggerations.
#Text=I have seen in a work on Ceylon the miserable\r
#Text=little acid berry of the rattan, which is no larger than a currant,\r
#Text=described as a fruit; hawthorn berries might, with equal justice, be\r
#Text=classed among the fruits of Great Britain.
#Text=I will not attempt to describe these paltry productions in detail;\r
#Text=there is necessarily a great variety throughout the island, but their\r
#Text=insignificance does not entitle them to a description which would raise\r
#Text=them far above their real merit.
#Text=It is nevertheless most useful to a sportsman in Ceylon to possess a\r
#Text=sufficient stock of botanical information for his personal convenience.
#Text=A man may be lost in the jungles or hard up for provisions in some\r
#Text=out-of-the-way place, where, if he has only a saucepan, he can\r
#Text=generally procure something eatable in the way of herbs.
#Text=It is not to\r
#Text=be supposed, however, that he would succeed in making a good dinner;\r
#Text=the reader may at any time procure something similar in England by\r
#Text=restricting himself to nettle-tops--an economical but not a fattening\r
#Text=vegetable.
#Text=Anything, however simple, is better than an empty stomach,\r
#Text=and when the latter is positively empty it is wonderful how the\r
#Text=appetite welcomes the most miserable fare.
#Text=At Newera Ellia the jungles would always produce a supply for a soupe\r
#Text=maigré.
#Text=There is an esculent nillho which grows in the forest in the\r
#Text=bottoms of the swampy ravines.
#Text=This is a most succulent plant, which\r
#Text=grows to the height or length of about seven feet, as its great weight\r
#Text=keeps it close to the ground.
#Text=It is so brittle that it snaps like a\r
#Text=cucumber when struck by a stick, and it bears a delicate, dark-blue\r
#Text=blossom.
#Text=When stewed, it is as tender as the vegetable marrow, but its\r
#Text=flavor approaches more closely to that of the cucumber.
#Text=Wild ginger\r
#Text=also abounds in the forests.
#Text=This is a coarse variety of the "amomum\r
#Text=zintgiber."
#Text=The leaves, which spring from the ground, attain a height\r
#Text=of seven or eight feet; a large, crimson, fleshy blossom also springs\r
#Text=from the ground in the centre of the surrounding leaf-stems.
#Text=The root\r
#Text=is coarse, large, but wanting in fine flavor, although the young tubers\r
#Text=are exceedingly tender and delicate.
#Text=This is the favorite food of\r
#Text=elephants on the Ceylon mountains; but it is a curious fact that they\r
#Text=invariably reject the leaves, which any one would suppose would be\r
#Text=their choicest morsel, as they are both succulent and plentiful.
#Text=The\r
#Text=elephants simply use them as a handle for tearing up the roots, which\r
#Text=they bite off and devour, throwing the leaves on one side.
#Text=The wild parsnip is also indigenous to the plains on the mountains.
#Text=As\r
#Text=usual with most wild plants of this class, it has little or no root,\r
#Text=but runs to leaf.
#Text=The seeds are very highly flavored, and are gathered\r
#Text=by the natives for their curries.
#Text=There is, likewise, a beautiful orchidaceous plant, which is very\r
#Text=common throughout the patinas on the mountains, and which produces the\r
#Text=very finest quality of arrowroot.
#Text=So much is this valued in the Nepaul\r
#Text=country in India, that I have been assured by a person well acquainted\r
#Text=with that locality, that this quality of arrowroot is usually sold for\r
#Text=its weight in rupees.
#Text=In vain have I explained this to the Cingalese;\r
#Text=they will not attempt its preparation because their fathers did not eat\r
#Text=it; and yet these same men will walk forty miles to cut a bundle of\r
#Text=sticks of the galla gaha tree for driving buffaloes!
#Text=--their fathers did\r
#Text=this, and therefore they do it.
#Text=Thus this beautiful plant is only\r
#Text=appreciated by those whose instinct leads them to its discovery.
#Text=The\r
#Text=wild hogs plough up the patinas and revel in this delicate food.
#Text=The\r
#Text=plant itself is almost lost in the rank herbage of the patinas, but its\r
#Text=beautiful pink, hyacinth-shaped blossom attracts immediate attention.
#Text=Few plants combine beauty of appearance, scent and utility, but this is\r
#Text=the perfection of each quality--nothing can surpass the delicacy and\r
#Text=richness of its perfume.
#Text=It has two small bulbs about an inch below\r
#Text=the surface of the earth, and these, when broken, exhibit a highly\r
#Text=granulated texture, semi-transparent like half-boiled sago.
#Text=From these\r
#Text=bulbs the arrowroot is produced by pounding them in water and drying\r
#Text=the precipitated farina in the sun.
#Text=There are several beautiful varieties of orchidaceous plants upon the\r
#Text=mountains; among others, several species of the dendrobium.
#Text=Its rich\r
#Text=yellow flowers hang in clusters from a withered tree, the only sign of\r
#Text=life upon a giant trunk decayed, like a wreath upon a grave.
#Text=The scent\r
#Text=of this flower is well known as most delicious; one plant will perfume\r
#Text=a large room.
#Text=There is one variety of this tribe in the neighborhood of Newera Ellia,\r
#Text=which is certainly unknown in English collections.
#Text=It blossoms in\r
#Text=April; the flowers are a bright lilac, and I could lay my band upon it\r
#Text=at any time, as I have never seen it but in one spot, where it\r
#Text=flourishes in profusion.
#Text=This is about fourteen miles from Newera\r
#Text=Ellia, and I have never yet collected a specimen, as I have invariably\r
#Text=been out hunting whenever I have met with it.
#Text=The black pepper  is also indigenous throughout Ceylon.
#Text=At Newera\r
#Text=Ellia the leaves of this vine are highly pungent, although at this\r
#Text=elevation it does not produce fruit.
#Text=A very short distance toward a\r
#Text=lower elevation effects a marked change, as within seven miles it\r
#Text=fruits in great perfection.
#Text=At a similar altitude, the wild nutmeg is very common throughout the\r
#Text=forests.
#Text=This fruit is a perfect anomaly.
#Text=The tree is entirely\r
#Text=different to that of the cultivated species.
#Text=The latter is small,\r
#Text=seldom exceeding the size of an apple-tree, and bearing a light green\r
#Text=myrtle-shaped leaf, which is not larger than that of a peach.
#Text=The wild\r
#Text=species, on the contrary, is a large forest tree, with leaves equal in\r
#Text=size to those of the horse chestnut; nevertheless, it produces a\r
#Text=perfect nutmeg.
#Text=There is the outer rind of fleshy texture, like an\r
#Text=unripe peach; enclosed within is the nutlike shell, enveloped in the\r
#Text=crimson network of mace, and within the shell is the nutmeg itself.
#Text=All this is perfect enough, but, alas, the grand desideratum is\r
#Text=wanting--it has no flavor or aroma whatever.
#Text=It is a gross imposition on the part of Nature; a most stingy trick\r
#Text=upon the public, and a regular do.
#Text=The mace has no taste whatever, and\r
#Text=the nutmeg has simply a highly acrid and pungent taste, without any\r
#Text=spicy flavor, but merely abounding in a rank and disagreeable oil.
#Text=The\r
#Text=latter is so plentiful that I am astonished it has not been\r
#Text=experimented upon, especially by the natives, who are great adepts in\r
#Text=expressing oils from many substances.
#Text=Those most common in Ceylon are the cocoa-nut and gingerly oils.
#Text=The\r
#Text=former is one of the grand staple commodities of the island; the latter\r
#Text=is the produce of a small grain, grown exclusively by the natives.
#Text=But, in addition to these, there are various other oils manufactured by\r
#Text=the Cingalese.
#Text=These are the cinnamon oil, castor oil, margosse oil,\r
#Text=mee oil, kenar oil, meeheeria oil; and both clove and lemon-grass oil\r
#Text=are prepared by Europeans.
#Text=The first, which is the cinnamon oil, is more properly a kind of\r
#Text=vegetable wax, being of the consistence of stearine.
#Text=This is prepared\r
#Text=from the berries of the cinnamon shrubs which are boiled in water until\r
#Text=the catty substance or so-called oil, floats upon the surface; this is\r
#Text=then skimmed off and, when a sufficient quantity is collected, it is\r
#Text=boiled down until all watery particles are evaporated, and the melted\r
#Text=fat is turned out into a shallow vessel to cool.
#Text=It has a pleasant,\r
#Text=though, perhaps, a rather faint aromatic smell, and is very delicious\r
#Text=as an adjunct in the culinary art.
#Text=In addition to this it possesses\r
#Text=gentle aperient properties, which render it particularly wholesome.
#Text=Castor oil is also obtained by the natives by boiling, and it is\r
#Text=accordingly excessively rank after long keeping.
#Text=The castor-oil plant\r
#Text=is a perfect weed throughout Ceylon, being one of the few useful shrubs\r
#Text=that will flourish in such poor soil without cultivation.
#Text=Margosse oil is extracted from the fruit of a tree of that name.
#Text=It has\r
#Text=an extremely fetid and disagreeable smell, which will effectually\r
#Text=prevent the contact of flies or any other insect.
#Text=On this account it\r
#Text=is a valuable preventive to the attacks of flies upon open wounds, in\r
#Text=addition to which it possesses powerful healing properties.
#Text=Mee oil is obtained from the fruit of the mee tree.
#Text=This fruit is\r
#Text=about the size of an apricot, and is extremely rich in its produce; but\r
#Text=the oil is of a coarse description, and is simply used by the natives\r
#Text=for their rude lamps.
#Text=Kenar oil and meeheeria oil are equally coarse,\r
#Text=and are quite unfit for any but native purposes.
#Text=Lemon-grass oil, which is known in commerce as citronella oil, is a\r
#Text=delightful extract from the rank lemon grass, which covers most of' the\r
#Text=hillsides in the more open districts of Ceylon.
#Text=An infusion of the\r
#Text=grass is subsequently distilled; the oil is then discovered on the\r
#Text=surface.
#Text=This is remarkably pure, with a most pungent aroma.
#Text=If\r
#Text=rubbed upon the skin, it will prevent the attacks of insects while its\r
#Text=perfume remains; but the oil is so volatile that the scent quickly\r
#Text=evaporates and the spell is broken.
#Text=Clove oil is extracted from the leaves of the cinnamon tree, and not\r
#Text=from cloves, as its name would imply.
#Text=The process is very similar to\r
#Text=that employed in the manufacture of citronella oil.
#Text=Cinnamon is indigenous throughout the jungles of Ceylon.
#Text=Even at the\r
#Text=high elevation of Newera Ellia, it is one of the most common woods, and\r
#Text=it grows to the dimensions of a forest tree, the trunk being usually\r
#Text=about three feet in circumference.
#Text=At Newera Ellia it loses much of\r
#Text=its fine flavor, although it is still highly aromatic.
#Text=This tree flourishes in a white quartz sandy soil, and in its\r
#Text=cultivated state is never allowed to exceed the dimensions of a bush,\r
#Text=being pruned down close to the ground every year.
#Text=This system of close\r
#Text=cutting induces the growth of a large number of shoots, in the same\r
#Text=manner that withes are produced in England.
#Text=Every twelve months these shoots attain the length of six or seven\r
#Text=feet, and the thickness of a man's finger.
#Text=In the interim, the only\r
#Text=cultivation required is repeated cleaning.
#Text=The whole plantation is cut\r
#Text=down at the proper period, and the sticks are then stripped of their\r
#Text=bark by the peelers.
#Text=These men are called "chalias," and their labor\r
#Text=is confined to this particular branch.
#Text=The season being over, they pass\r
#Text=the remaining portion of the year in idleness, their earnings during\r
#Text=one crop being sufficient to supply their trifling wants until the\r
#Text=ensuing harvest.
#Text=Their practice in this employment naturally renders them particularly\r
#Text=expert, and in far less time than is occupied in the description they\r
#Text=run a sharp knife longitudinally along a stick, and at once divest it\r
#Text=of the bark.
#Text=On the following day the strips of bark are scraped so as\r
#Text=entirely to remove the outer cuticle.
#Text=One strip is then laid within\r
#Text=the other, which, upon becoming dry, contract, and form a series of\r
#Text=enclosed pipes.
#Text=It is subsequently packed in bales, and carefully sewed\r
#Text=up in double sacks for exportation.
#Text=The essential oil of cinnamon is usually made from the refuse of the\r
#Text=crop; but the quantity produced, in proportion to the weight of\r
#Text=cinnamon, is exceedingly small, being about five ounces of oil to half\r
#Text=a hundred-weight of the spice.
#Text=Although the cinnamon appears to require no more than a common quartz\r
#Text=sand for its production, it is always cultivated with the greatest\r
#Text=success where the subsoil is light, dry and of a loamy quality.
#Text=The appearance of the surface soil is frequently very deceitful.
#Text=It is\r
#Text=not uncommon to see a forest of magnificent trees growing in soil of\r
#Text=apparently pure sand, which will not even produce the underwood with\r
#Text=which Ceylon forests are generally choked.
#Text=In such an instance the\r
#Text=appearance of the trees is unusually grand as their whole length and\r
#Text=dimensions are exposed to view, and their uniting crowns throw a sombre\r
#Text=shade over the barren ground beneath.
#Text=It is not to be supposed that\r
#Text=these mighty specimens of vegetation are supported by the poor sandy\r
#Text=soil upon the surface; their tap-roots strike down into some richer\r
#Text=stratum, from which their nourishment is derived.
#Text=These forests are not common in Ceylon; their rarity accordingly\r
#Text=enhances their beauty.
#Text=The largest English oak would be a mere pigmy\r
#Text=among the giants of these wilds, whose stature is so wonderful that the\r
#Text=eye never becomes tired of admiration.
#Text=Often have I halted on my\r
#Text=journey to ride around and admire the prodigious height and girth of\r
#Text=these trees.
#Text=Their beautiful proportions render them the more\r
#Text=striking; there are no gnarled and knotty stems, such as we are\r
#Text=accustomed to admire in the ancient oaks and beeches of England, but\r
#Text=every trunk rises like a mast from the earth, perfectly free from\r
#Text=branches for ninety or a hundred feet, straight as an arrow, each tree\r
#Text=forming a dark pillar to support its share of the rich canopy above,\r
#Text=which constitutes a roof perfectly impervious to the sun.
#Text=It is\r
#Text=difficult to guess the actual height of these forest trees; but I have\r
#Text=frequently noticed that it is impossible to shoot a bird on the higher\r
#Text=branches with No. 5 shot.
#Text=It is much to be regretted that the want of the means of transport\r
#Text=renders the timber of these forests perfectly valueless.
#Text=From age to\r
#Text=age these magnificent trees remain in their undisturbed solitudes,\r
#Text=gradually increasing in their apparently endless growth, and towering\r
#Text=above the dark vistas of everlasting silence.
#Text=No on can imagine the\r
#Text=utter stillness which pervades these gloomy shades.
#Text=There is a\r
#Text=mysterious effect produced by the total absence of animal life.
#Text=In the\r
#Text=depths of these forests I have stood and listened for some sound until\r
#Text=my cars tingled with overstrained attention; not a chirp of a bird, not\r
#Text=the hum of an insect, but the mouth of Nature is sealed.
#Text=Not a breath\r
#Text=of air has rustled a leaf, not even a falling fruit has broken the\r
#Text=spell of silence; the undying verdure, the freshness of each tree, even\r
#Text=in its mysterious age, create an idea of eternal vegetation, and the\r
#Text=silvery yet dim light adds to the charm of the fairylike solitude which\r
#Text=gradually steals over the senses.
#Text=I have ridden for fifteen or twenty miles through one of these forests\r
#Text=without hearing a sound, except that of my horse's hoof occasionally\r
#Text=striking against a root.
#Text=Neither beast nor bird is to be seen except\r
#Text=upon the verge.
#Text=The former has no food upon such barren ground; and\r
#Text=the latter can find no berries, as the earth is sunless and free from\r
#Text=vegetation.
#Text=Not even monkeys are to be seen, although the trees must\r
#Text=produce fruit and seed.
#Text=Everything appears to have deserted the\r
#Text=country, and to have yielded it as the sole territory of Nature on a\r
#Text=stupendous scale.
#Text=The creepers lie serpent-like along the ground to the\r
#Text=thickness of a man's waist, and, rearing their twisted forms on high,\r
#Text=they climb the loftiest trees, hanging in festoons from stern to stem\r
#Text=like the cables of a line-of-battle-ship, and extending from tree to\r
#Text=tree for many hundred yards; now felling to the earth and striking a\r
#Text=fresh root; then, with increased energy, remounting the largest trunks,\r
#Text=and forming a labyrinth of twisted ropes along the ceiling of the\r
#Text=forest.
#Text=From these creepers hang the sabre-beans.
#Text=Everything seems on\r
#Text=a supernatural scale--the bean-pod four feet or more in length, by\r
#Text=three inches in breadth; the beans two inches in diameter.
#Text=Here may be seen the most valuable woods of Ceylon.
#Text=The ebony grows in\r
#Text=great perfection and large quantity.
#Text=This tree is at once\r
#Text=distinguished from the surrounding stems by its smaller diameter and\r
#Text=its sooty trunk.
#Text=The bark is crisp, jet black, and has the appearance\r
#Text=of being charred.
#Text=Beneath the bark the wood is perfectly white until\r
#Text=the heart is reached, which is the fine black ebony of commerce.
#Text=Here\r
#Text=also, equally immovable, the calamander is growing, neglected and\r
#Text=unknown.
#Text=This is the most esteemed of all Ceylon woods, and it is so\r
#Text=rare that it realizes a fancy price.
#Text=It is something similar to the\r
#Text=finest walnut, the color being a rich hazel brown, mottled and striped\r
#Text=with irregular black marks.
#Text=It is superior to walnut in the extreme\r
#Text=closeness of the grain and the richness of its color.
#Text=There are upward of eighty different woods produced in Ceylon, which\r
#Text=are made use of for various purposes; but of these many are very\r
#Text=inferior.
#Text=Those most appreciated are--\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Calamander, Ebony, chiefly used for furniture and cabinet work.
#Text=Satin-wood, Suria (the tulip tree).
#Text=Tamarind.
#Text=Jackwood.
#Text=Halmileel.
#Text=Cocoa-nut.
#Text=Palmyra.
#Text=The suria is an elegant tree, bearing a beautiful yellow blossom\r
#Text=something similar to a tulip, from which it derives its name.
#Text=The wood\r
#Text=is of an extremely close texture and of a reddish-brown color.
#Text=It is\r
#Text=exceedingly tough, and it is chiefly used for making the spokes of\r
#Text=wheels.
#Text=The tamarind is a fine, dark red wood, mottled with black marks; but it\r
#Text=is not in general use, as the tree is too valuable to be felled for the\r
#Text=sake of its timber.
#Text=This is one of the handsomest trees of the tropics,\r
#Text=growing to a very large size, the branches widely spreading, something\r
#Text=like the cedars of Lebanon.
#Text=Jackwood is a coarse imitation of mahogany, and is used for a variety\r
#Text=of purposes, especially for making cheap furniture.
#Text=The latter is not\r
#Text=only economical, but exceedingly durable, and is manufactured at so low\r
#Text=a rate that a moderate-sized house might be entirely furnished with it\r
#Text=for a hundred and fifty pounds.
#Text=The fruit of the jack grows from the trunk and branches of the tree,\r
#Text=and when ripe it weighs about twenty pounds.
#Text=The rind is rough, and\r
#Text=when cut it exposes a yellow, pulpy mass.
#Text=This is formed of an\r
#Text=infinite number of separate divisions of fleshy matter, which severally\r
#Text=enclose an oval nut.
#Text=The latter are very good when roasted, having a\r
#Text=close resemblance to a chestnut.
#Text=The pulp, which is the real fruit, is\r
#Text=not usually eaten by Europeans on account of its peculiar odor.
#Text=This\r
#Text=perfume is rather difficult to describe, but when a rainy day in London\r
#Text=crams an omnibus with well-soaked and steaming multitudes, the\r
#Text=atmosphere in the vehicle somewhat approaches to the smell of the\r
#Text=jack-fruit.
#Text=The halmileel is one of the most durable and useful woods\r
#Text=in Ceylon, and is almost the only kind that is thoroughly adapted for\r
#Text=making staves for casks.
#Text=Of late years the great increase of the\r
#Text=oil-trade has brought this wood into general request, consequent upon\r
#Text=the increased demand for casks.
#Text=So extensive and general is the\r
#Text=present demand for this wood that the natives are continually occupied\r
#Text=in conveying it from certain districts which a few years ago were\r
#Text=utterly neglected.
#Text=Unfortunately, the want of roads and the means of\r
#Text=transport confine their operations to the banks of rivers, down which\r
#Text=the logs are floated at the proper season.
#Text=I recollect some eight years ago crossing the Mahawelli river upon a\r
#Text=raft which my coolies had hastily constructed, and reaching a miserable\r
#Text=village near Monampitya, in the extreme north of the Veddah country.
#Text=The river is here about four hundred paces wide, and, in the rainy\r
#Text=season a fine volume of water rolls along in a rapid stream toward\r
#Text=Trincomalee, at which place it meets the sea.
#Text=I was struck it the time\r
#Text=with the magnificent timber in the forests on its banks, and no less\r
#Text=surprised that with the natural facilities of transport it should be\r
#Text=neglected.
#Text=Two years ago I crossed at this same spot, and I remarked\r
#Text=the wonderful change which a steady demand had effected in this wild\r
#Text=country.
#Text=Extensive piles of halmileel logs were collected along the\r
#Text=banks of the river, while the forests were strewed with felled trees in\r
#Text=preparation for floating down the stream.
#Text=A regular demand usually\r
#Text=ensures a regular supply, which could not be better exemplified than in\r
#Text=this case.
#Text=Among fancy woods the bread-fruit tree should not be omitted.
#Text=This is\r
#Text=something similar to the jack, but, like the tamarind, the value of the\r
#Text=produce saves the tree from destruction.
#Text=This tree does not attain a very large size, but its growth is\r
#Text=exceedingly regular and the foliage peculiarly rich and plentiful.
#Text=The\r
#Text=fruit is something similar in appearance to a small, unripe jack-fruit,\r
#Text=with an equally rough exterior.
#Text=In the opinion of most who have tasted\r
#Text=it, its virtues have been grossly exaggerated.
#Text=To my taste it is\r
#Text=perfectly uneatable, unless fried in thin slices with butter; it is\r
#Text=even then a bad imitation of fried potatoes.
#Text=The bark of this tree\r
#Text=produces a strong fibre, and a kind of very adhesive pitch is also\r
#Text=produced by decoction.
#Text=The cocoa-nut and palmyra woods at once introduce us to the palms of\r
#Text=Ceylon, the most useful and the most elegant class in vegetation.
#Text=For\r
#Text=upward of a hundred and twenty miles along the western and southern\r
#Text=coasts of Ceylon, one continuous line of cocoa-nut groves wave their\r
#Text=green leaves to the sea-breeze, without a single break, except where\r
#Text=some broad clear river cleaves the line of verdure as it meets the sea.
#Text=Ceylon is rich in palms, including the following varieties: The\r
#Text=Cocoa-nut.
#Text=The Palmyra.
#Text=The Kittool.
#Text=The Areca The Date.
#Text=The Sago.
#Text=The\r
#Text=Talipot.
#Text=The wonderful productions of this tribe can only be appreciated by\r
#Text=those who thoroughly understand the habits and necessities of the\r
#Text=natives; and, upon examination, it will be seen that Nature has opened\r
#Text=wide her bountiful hand, and in the midst of a barren soil she has\r
#Text=still remembered and supplied the wants of the inhabitants.
#Text=As the stream issued from the rock in the wilderness, to the cocoa-nut\r
#Text=tree yields a pure draught from a dry and barren land; a cup of water\r
#Text=to the temperate and thirsty traveler; a cup of cream from the pressed\r
#Text=kernel; a cup of refreshing and sparkling toddy to the early riser; a\r
#Text=cup of arrack to the hardened spirit-drinker, and a cup of oil, by the\r
#Text=light of which I now extol its merits-five separate and distinct\r
#Text=liquids from the same tree!
#Text=A green or unripe cocoa-nut contains about a pint of a sweetish water.
#Text=In the hottest weather this is deliciously cool, in comparison to the\r
#Text=heat of the atmosphere.
#Text=The ripe nut, when scraped into a pulp by a little serrated,\r
#Text=semi-circular iron instrument, is squeezed in a cloth by the hand, and\r
#Text=about a quarter of a pint of delicious thick cream, highly flavored by\r
#Text=cocoa-nut, is then expressed.
#Text=This forms the chief ingredient in a\r
#Text=Cingalese curry, from which it entirely derives its richness and fine\r
#Text=flavor.
#Text=The toddy is the sap which would nourish and fructify the blossom and\r
#Text=young nuts, were it allowed to accomplish its duties.
#Text=The toddy-drawer\r
#Text=binds into one rod the numerous shoots, which are garnished with embryo\r
#Text=nuts, and he then cuts off the ends, leaving an abrupt and brush-like\r
#Text=termination.
#Text=Beneath this he secures an earthen chatty, which will\r
#Text=hold about a gallon.
#Text=This remains undisturbed for twenty-four hours,\r
#Text=from sunrise to sunrise on the following morning; the toddy-drawer then\r
#Text=reascends the tree, and lowers he chatty by a line to an assistant\r
#Text=below, who empties the contents into a larger vessel, and the chatty is\r
#Text=replaced under the productive branch, which continues to yield for\r
#Text=about a month.
#Text=When first drawn the toddy has the appearance of thin milk and water,\r
#Text=with a combined flavor of milk and soda-water, with a tinge of\r
#Text=cocoa-nut.
#Text=It is then very pleasant and refreshing, but in a few hours\r
#Text=after sunrise a great charts takes place, and the rapidity of the\r
#Text=transition from the vinous to the acetous fermentation is so great that\r
#Text=by midday it resembles a poor and rather acid cider.
#Text=It now possesses\r
#Text=intoxicating properties, and the natives accordingly indulge in it to\r
#Text=some extent; but from its flavor and decided acidity I should have\r
#Text=thought the stomach would be affected some time before the head.
#Text=From this fermented toddy the arrack is procured by simple distillation.
#Text=This spirit, to my taste, is more palatable than most distilled\r
#Text=liquors, having a very decided and peculiar flavor.
#Text=It is a little\r
#Text=fiery when new, but as water soon quenches fire, it is not spared by\r
#Text=the native retailers, whose arrack would be of a most innocent\r
#Text=character were it not for their infamous addition of stupefying drugs\r
#Text=and hot peppers.
#Text=The toddy contains a large proportion of saccharine, without which the\r
#Text=vinous fermentation could not take place.
#Text=This is procured by\r
#Text=evaporation in boiling, on the same principle that sugar is produced\r
#Text=from cane-juice.
#Text=The syrup is then poured into small saucers to cool,\r
#Text=and it shortly assumes the consistence of hardened sugar.
#Text=This is\r
#Text=known in Ceylon as "jaggery," and is manufactured exclusively by the\r
#Text=natives.
#Text=Cocoa-nut oil is now one of the greatest exports of Ceylon, and within\r
#Text=the last few years the trade has increased to an unprecedented extent.
#Text=In the two years of 1849 and 1850, the exports of cocoa-nut oil did not\r
#Text=exceed four hundred and forty-three thousand six hundred gallons, while\r
#Text=in the year 1853 they had increased to one million thirty-three\r
#Text=thousand nine hundred gallons; the trade being more than quadrupled in\r
#Text=three years.
#Text=The manufacture of the oil is most simple.
#Text=The kernel is taken from\r
#Text=the nut, and being divided, it is exposed to the sun until all the\r
#Text=watery particles are evaporated.
#Text=The kernel thus dried is known as\r
#Text="copperah."
#Text=This is then pressed in a mill, and the oil flows into a\r
#Text=reservoir.
#Text=This oil, although clear and limpid in the tropics, hardens to the\r
#Text=consistence of lard at any temperature below 72 Fahrenheit.
#Text=Thus it\r
#Text=requires a second preparation on its arrival in England.
#Text=There it is\r
#Text=spread upon mats (formed of coir) to the thickness of an inch, and then\r
#Text=covered by a similar protection.
#Text=These fat sandwiches are two feet\r
#Text=square, and being piled one upon the other to a height of about six\r
#Text=feet in an hydraulic press, are subjected to a pressure of some hundred\r
#Text=tons.
#Text=This disengages the pure oleaginous parts from the more\r
#Text=insoluble portions, and the fat residue, being increased in hardness by\r
#Text=its extra density, is mixed with stearine, and by a variety of\r
#Text=preparations is converted into candles.
#Text=The pure oil thus expressed is\r
#Text=that known in the shops as cocoa-nut oil.
#Text=The cultivation of the cocoa-nut tree is now carried to a great extent,\r
#Text=both by natives and Europeans; by the former it is grown for a variety\r
#Text=of purposes, but by the latter its profits are confined to oil, coir\r
#Text=and poonac.
#Text=The latter is the refuse Of the nut after the oil has been\r
#Text=expressed, and corresponds in its uses to the linseed-oil cake of\r
#Text=England, being chiefly employed for fattening cattle, pigs and poultry.
#Text=The preparation of coir is a dirty and offensive occupation.
#Text=The husk\r
#Text=of the cocoa-nut is thrown into tanks of water, until the woody or\r
#Text=pithy matter is loosened by fermentation from the coir fibre.
#Text=The\r
#Text=stench of putrid vegetable matter arising from these heaps must be\r
#Text=highly deleterious.
#Text=Subsequently the husks are beaten and the fibre is\r
#Text=separated and dried.
#Text=Coir rope is useful on account of its durability\r
#Text=and power of resisting decay during long immersion.
#Text=In the year 1853,\r
#Text=twenty-three hundred and eighty tons of coir were exported from Ceylon.
#Text=The great drawback to the commencement of a cocoa-nut plantation is the\r
#Text=total uncertainty of the probable alteration in the price of oil during\r
#Text=the interval of eleven years which must elapse before the estate comes\r
#Text=into bearing.
#Text=In this era of invention, when improvements in every\r
#Text=branch of science follow each other with such rapid strides, it is\r
#Text=always a dangerous speculation to make any outlay that will remain so\r
#Text=long invested without producing a return.
#Text=Who can be so presumptuous\r
#Text=as to predict the changes of future years?
#Text=Oil may have ceased to be\r
#Text=the common medium of light--our rooms may be illumined by electricity,\r
#Text=or from fifty other sources which now are never dreamed of.
#Text=In the\r
#Text=mean time, the annual outlay during eleven years is an additional\r
#Text=incubus upon the prime cost of the plantation, which, at the expiration\r
#Text=of this term, may be reduced to one-tenth of its present value.
#Text=The cocoa-nut tree requires a sandy and well-drained soil; and although\r
#Text=it flourishes where no other tree will grow, it welcomes a soil of a\r
#Text=richer quality and produces fruit in proportion.
#Text=Eighty nuts per annum\r
#Text=are about the average income from a healthy tree in full bearing, but\r
#Text=this, of course, depends much upon the locality.
#Text=This palm delights in\r
#Text=the sea-breeze, and never attains the same perfection inland that it\r
#Text=does in the vicinity of the coast.
#Text=There are several varieties, and\r
#Text=that which is considered superior is the yellow species, called the\r
#Text="king cocoanut."
#Text=I have seen this on the Maldive Islands in great\r
#Text=perfection.
#Text=There it is the prevailing description.
#Text=At the Seychelles, there is a variety peculiar to those islands,\r
#Text=differing entirely in appearance from the common cocoa-nut.
#Text=It is\r
#Text=fully twice the size, and is shaped like a kidney that is laid open.
#Text=This is called by the French the "coco de mer" from the large numbers\r
#Text=that are found floating in the sea in the neighborhood of the islands.
#Text=The wood of the cocoa-nut tree is strong and durable; it is a dark\r
#Text=brown, traversed by longitudinal black lines.
#Text=There are three varieties of toddy-producing palms in Ceylon; these are\r
#Text=the cocoa-nut, the kittool and the palmyra.
#Text=The latter produces the\r
#Text=finest quality of jaggery.
#Text=This cannot be easily distinguished from\r
#Text=crumbled sugar-candy which it exactly resembles in flavor, The wood of\r
#Text=the palmyra is something similar to the cocoa-nut, but it is of a\r
#Text=superior quality, and is much used for rafters, being durable and of\r
#Text=immense strength.
#Text=The kittool is a very sombre and peculiar palm.
#Text=Its crest very much\r
#Text=resembles the drooping plume upon a hearse, and the foliage is a dark\r
#Text=green with a tinge of gray.
#Text=The wood of this palm is almost black,\r
#Text=being apparently a mass of longitudinal strips, or coarse linen of\r
#Text=whalebone running close together from the top to the root of the tree.
#Text=This is the toughest and most pliable of all the palm-woods, and is\r
#Text=principally used by the natives in making "pingos."
#Text=These are flat bows\r
#Text=about eight feet in length, and are used by the Cingalese for carrying\r
#Text=loads upon the shoulder.
#Text=The weight is slung at either end of the\r
#Text=pingo, and the elasticity of the wood accommodates itself to the spring\r
#Text=of each step, thereby reducing the dead weight of the load.
#Text=In this\r
#Text=manner a stout Cingalese will carry and travel with eighty pounds if\r
#Text=working on his own account, or with fifty if hired for a journey.
#Text=A\r
#Text=Cingalese will carry a much heavier weight than an ordinary Malabar, as\r
#Text=he is a totally different man in form and strength.
#Text=In fact, the\r
#Text=Cingalese are generally a compactly built and well-limbed race, while\r
#Text=the Malabar is a man averaging full a stone lighter weight.
#Text=The most extraordinary in the list of palms is the talipot.
#Text=The crest\r
#Text=of this beautiful tree is adorned by a crown of nearly circular,\r
#Text=fan-shaped leaves of so touch and durable a texture that they are sewn\r
#Text=together by the natives for erecting portable tents or huts.
#Text=The\r
#Text=circumference of each leaf at the extreme edge is from twenty to thirty\r
#Text=feet, and even this latter size is said to be frequently exceeded.
#Text=Every Cingalese throughout the Kandian district is provided with a\r
#Text=section of one of these leaves, which forms a kind of fan about six\r
#Text=feet in length.
#Text=This is carried in the hand, and is only spread in\r
#Text=case of rain, when it forms an impervious roofing of about three feet\r
#Text=in width at the broad extremity.
#Text=Four or five of these sections will\r
#Text=form a circular roof for a small hut, which resembles a large umbrella\r
#Text=or brobdignag mushroom.
#Text=There is a great peculiarity in the talipot palm.
#Text=Is blossoms only\r
#Text=once in a long period of years, and after this it dies.
#Text=No flower can\r
#Text=equal the elegance and extraordinary dimensions of this blossom; its\r
#Text=size is proportionate to its leaves, and it usurps the place of the\r
#Text=faded crest of green, forming a magnificent crown or plume of\r
#Text=snow-white ostrich feathers, which stand upon the summit of the tall\r
#Text=stem as though they were the natural head of the palm.
#Text=There is an interesting phenomenon at the period of flowering.
#Text=The\r
#Text=great plume already described, prior to its appearing in bloom, is\r
#Text=packed in a large case or bud, about four feet long.
#Text=In this case the\r
#Text=blossom comes to maturity, at which time the tightened cuticle of the\r
#Text=bard can no longer sustain the pressure of the expanding flower.
#Text=It\r
#Text=suddenly bursts with a loud report, and the beautiful plume, freed from\r
#Text=its imprisonment, ascends at this signal and rapidly unfolds its\r
#Text=feathers, towering above the drooping leaves which are hastening to\r
#Text=decay.
#Text=The areca is a palm of great elegance; it rises to a height of about\r
#Text=eighty feet, and a rich feathery crest adorns the summit.
#Text=This is the\r
#Text=most delicate stem of all the palm tribe; that of a tree of eighty feet\r
#Text=in length would not exceed five inches in diameter.
#Text=Nevertheless, I\r
#Text=have never seen an areca palm overturned by a storm; they bow\r
#Text=gracefully to the wind, and the extreme elasticity of the wood secures\r
#Text=them from destruction.
#Text=This tree produces the commonly-called "betel-nut," but more properly\r
#Text=the areca-nut.
#Text=They grow in clusters beneath the crest of the palm, in\r
#Text=a similar manner to the cocoa-nut; but the tree is more prolific, as it\r
#Text=produces about two hundred nuts per annum.
#Text=The latter are very similar\r
#Text=to large nutmegs both in size and appearance, and, like the cocoa-nut,\r
#Text=they are enclosed in an outer husk of a fibrous texture.
#Text=The consumption of these nuts may be imagined when it is explained that\r
#Text=every native is perpetually chewing a mixture of this nut and betel\r
#Text=leaf.
#Text=Every man carries a betel bag, which contains the following list\r
#Text=of treasures: a quantity of areca-nuts, a parcel of betel leaves, a\r
#Text=roll of tobacco, a few pieces of ginger, an instrument similar to\r
#Text=pruning scissors and a brass or silver case (according to the wealth of\r
#Text=the individual) full of chunam paste--viz., a fine lime produced from\r
#Text=burnt coral, slacked.
#Text=This case very much resembles an old-fashioned\r
#Text=warming-pan breed of watch and chateleine, as numerous little spoons\r
#Text=for scooping out the chunam are attached to it by chains.
#Text=The betel is a species of pepper, the leaf of which very much resembles\r
#Text=that of the black pepper, but is highly aromatic and pungent.
#Text=It is\r
#Text=cultivated to a very large extent by the natives, and may be seen\r
#Text=climbing round poles and trees in every garden.
#Text=It has been said by some authors that the betel has powerful narcotic\r
#Text=properties, but, on the contrary, its stimulating qualities have a\r
#Text=directly opposite effect.
#Text=Those who have attributed this supposed\r
#Text=property to the betel leaf must have indulged in a regular native\r
#Text="chew" as an experiment, and have nevertheless been ignorant of the\r
#Text=mixture.
#Text=We will make up a native "chew" after the most approved fashion, and\r
#Text=the reader shall judge for himself in which ingredient the narcotic\r
#Text=principle is displayed.
#Text=Take a betel leaf, and upon this spread a piece of chunam as large as a\r
#Text=pea; then with the pruning scissors cut three very thin slices of\r
#Text=areca-nut, and lay them in the leaf; next, add a small piece of ginger;\r
#Text=and, lastly, a good-sized piece of tobacco.
#Text=Fold up this mixture in\r
#Text=another betel leaf in a compact little parcel, and it is fit for\r
#Text=promoting several hours' enjoyment in chewing, and spitting a\r
#Text=disgusting blood-red dye in every direction.
#Text=The latter is produced by\r
#Text=the areca-nut.
#Text=It is the tobacco which possesses the narcotic\r
#Text=principle; if this is omitted, the remaining ingredients are simple\r
#Text=stimulants.
#Text=The teeth of all natives are highly discolored by the perpetual\r
#Text=indulgence in this disgusting habit; nor is this the only effect\r
#Text=produced; cancer in the cheek is a common complaint among them,\r
#Text=supposed to be produced by the caustic lime which is so continually in\r
#Text=the mouth.
#Text=The exports of areca-nuts from Ceylon will give some idea of the supply\r
#Text=of palms.
#Text=In 1853 no less than three thousand tons were shipped from\r
#Text=this colony, valued at about 45,000 l.
#Text=The greater portion of these is\r
#Text=consumed in India.
#Text=Two varieties of palms remain to be described--the date and the sago.
#Text=The former is a miserable species, which does not exceed the height of\r
#Text=three to five feet, and the fruit is perfectly worthless.
#Text=The latter is indigenous throughout the jungles in Ceylon, but it is\r
#Text=neither cultivated, nor is the sago prepared from it.
#Text=The height of this palm does not exceed fifteen or twenty feet, and\r
#Text=even this is above the general average.
#Text=It grows in the greatest\r
#Text=profusion in the Veddah country.
#Text=The stem is rough and a continuation\r
#Text=of rings divides it into irregular sections.
#Text=The leaves are a rich\r
#Text=dark green, and very light and feathery, beneath which the nuts grow in\r
#Text=clusters similar to those of the areca palm.
#Text=The only use that the natives make of the produce of this tree is in\r
#Text=the preparation of flour from the nuts.
#Text=Even this is not very general,\r
#Text=which is much to be wondered at, as the farina is far superior in\r
#Text=flavor to that produced from most grains.
#Text=The natives ascribe intoxicating properties to the cakes made from this\r
#Text=flour; but I have certainly eaten a fair allowance at one time, and I\r
#Text=cannot say that I had the least sensation of elevation.
#Text=The nut, which is something similar to the areca in size, is nearly\r
#Text=white when divested of its outer husk, and this is soaked for about\r
#Text=twenty-four hours in water.
#Text=During this time a slight fermentation\r
#Text=takes place and the gas generated splits the nut open at a closed joint\r
#Text=like an acorn.
#Text=This fermentation may, perhaps, take some exhilarating\r
#Text=effect upon the natives' weak heads.
#Text=The nuts being partially softened by this immersion are dried in the\r
#Text=sun, and subsequently pounded into flour in a wooden mortar.
#Text=This flour\r
#Text=is sifted, and the coarser parts being separated, are again pounded\r
#Text=until a beautiful snow-white farina is produced.
#Text=This is made into a\r
#Text=dough by a proper admixture with water, and being formed into small\r
#Text=cakes, they are baked for about a quarter of an hour in a chatty.
#Text=The\r
#Text=fermentation which has already taken place in the nut has impregnated\r
#Text=the flower with a leaven; this, without any further addition, expands\r
#Text=the dough when in the oven, and the cake produced is very similar to a\r
#Text=crumpet, both in appearance and flavor.
#Text=The village in which I first tasted this preparation of the sago-nut\r
#Text=was a tolerable sample of such places, on the borders of the Veddah\r
#Text=country.
#Text=The population consisted of one old man and a corresponding\r
#Text=old woman, and one fine stout young man and five young women.
#Text=A host\r
#Text=of little children, who were so similar in height that they must have\r
#Text=been one litter, and three or four most miserable dogs and cats, were\r
#Text=additional tenants of the soi-disant village.
#Text=These people lived upon sago cakes, pumpkins, wild fruits and berries,\r
#Text=river fish and wild honey.
#Text=The latter is very plentiful throughout\r
#Text=Ceylon, and the natives are very expert in finding out the nests, by\r
#Text=watching the bees in their flight and following them up.
#Text=A bee-hunter\r
#Text=must be a most keen-sighted fellow, although there is not so much\r
#Text=difficulty in the pursuit as may at first appear.
#Text=No one can mistake\r
#Text=the flight of a bee en route home, if he has once observed him.
#Text=He is\r
#Text=no longer wandering from flower to flower in an uncertain course, but\r
#Text=he rushes through the air in a straight line for the nest.
#Text=If the\r
#Text=bee-hunter sees one bee thus speeding homeward, he watches the vacant\r
#Text=spot in the air, until assured of the direction by the successive\r
#Text=appearance of these insects, one following the other nearly every\r
#Text=second in their hurried race to the comb.
#Text=Keeping his eye upon the\r
#Text=passing bees, he follows them until he reaches the tree in which the\r
#Text=nest is found.
#Text=There are five varieties of bees in Ceylon; these are all honey-makers,\r
#Text=except the carpenter bee.
#Text=This species is entirely unlike a bee in all\r
#Text=its habits.
#Text=It is a bright tinsel-green color, and the size of a large\r
#Text=walnut, but shaped like the humble bees of England.
#Text=The month is armed\r
#Text=with a very powerful pair of mandibles, and the tail with a sting even\r
#Text=larger and more venomous than that of the hornet.
#Text=These carpenter bees\r
#Text=are exceedingly destructive, as they bore holes in beams and posts, in\r
#Text=which they lay their eggs, the larvae of which when hatched greedily\r
#Text=feed upon the timber.
#Text=The honey bees are of four very distinct varieties, each of which forms\r
#Text=its nest on a different principle.
#Text=The largest and most extensive\r
#Text=honey-maker is the "bambera".
#Text=This is nearly as large as a hornet, and\r
#Text=it forms its nest upon the bough of a tree, from which it lines like a\r
#Text=Cheshire cheese, being about the same thickness, but five or six inches\r
#Text=greater in diameter.
#Text=The honey of this bee is not so much esteemed as\r
#Text=that from the smaller varieties, as the flavor partakes too strongly of\r
#Text=the particular flower which the bee has frequented; thus in different\r
#Text=seasons the honey varies in flavor, and is sometimes so highly aperient\r
#Text=that it must be used with much caution.
#Text=This property is of course\r
#Text=derived from the flower which the bee prefers at that particular\r
#Text=season.
#Text=The wax of the comb is the purest and whitest of any kind\r
#Text=produced in Ceylon.
#Text=So partial are these bees to particular flowers\r
#Text=that they migrate from place to place at different periods in quest of\r
#Text=flowers which are then in bloom.
#Text=This is a very wonderful and inexplicable arrangement of Nature, when\r
#Text=it is considered that some flowers which particularly attract these\r
#Text=migrations only blossom once in "seven years."
#Text=This is the case at\r
#Text=Newera Ellia, where the nillho blossom induces such a general rush of\r
#Text=this particular bee to the district that the jungles are swarming with\r
#Text=them in every direction, although during the six preceding years hardly\r
#Text=a bee of the kind is to be met with.
#Text=There are many varieties of the nillho.
#Text=These vary from a tender dwarf\r
#Text=plant to the tall and heavy stern of the common nillho, which is nearly\r
#Text=as thick as a man's arm and about twenty feet high.
#Text=The next honey-maker is very similar in size and appearance to our\r
#Text=common hive bee in England.
#Text=This variety forms its nest in hollow\r
#Text=trees and in holes in rocks.
#Text=Another bee, similar in appearance, but\r
#Text=not more than half the size, suspends a most delicate comb to the twigs\r
#Text=of a tree.
#Text=This nest is no larger than an orange, but the honey of the\r
#Text=two latter varieties is of the finest quality, and quite equal in\r
#Text=flavor to the famed "miel vert" of the Isle de Burbon, although it has\r
#Text=not the delicate green tint which is so much esteemed in the latter.
#Text=The last of the Ceylon bees is the most tiny, although an equally\r
#Text=industrious workman.
#Text=He is a little smaller than our common house-fly,\r
#Text=and he builds his diminutive nest in the hollow of a tree, where the\r
#Text=entrance to his mansion is a hole no larger than would be made by a\r
#Text=lady's stiletto.
#Text=It would be a natural supposition that so delicate an insect would\r
#Text=produce a honey of corresponding purity, but instead of the expected\r
#Text=treasure we find a thick, black and rather pungent but highly aromatic\r
#Text=molasses.
#Text=The natives, having naturally coarse tastes and strong\r
#Text=stomachs, admire this honey beyond any other.
#Text=Many persons are\r
#Text=surprised at the trifling exports of wax from Ceylon.
#Text=In 1853 these\r
#Text=amounted to no more than one ton.
#Text=Cingalese are curious people, and do not trouble themselves about\r
#Text=exports; they waste or consume all the beeswax.
#Text=While we are contented\r
#Text=with the honey and carefully reject the comb, the native (in some\r
#Text=districts) crams his mouth with a large section, and giving it one or\r
#Text=two bites, he bolts the luscious morsel and begins another.
#Text=In this\r
#Text=manner immense quantities of this valuable article are annually wasted.
#Text=Some few of the natives in the poorest villages save a small quantity,\r
#Text=to exchange with the travelling Moormen for cotton cloths, etc., and in\r
#Text=this manner the trifling amount exported is collected.
#Text=During the honey year at Newera Ellia I gave a native permission to\r
#Text=hunt bees in my forests, on condition that he should bring me the wax.
#Text=Of course he stole the greater portion, but nevertheless, in a few\r
#Text=weeks he brought me seventy-two pounds' weight of well-cleaned and\r
#Text=perfectly white wax, which he had made up into balls about the size of\r
#Text=an eighteen-pound shot.
#Text=Thus, in a few weeks, one man had collected\r
#Text=about the thirtieth part of the annual export from Ceylon; or, allowing\r
#Text=that he stole at least one-half, this would amount to the fifteenth.
#Text=It would be a vain attempt to restrain these people from their fixed\r
#Text=habit; they would as soon think of refraining from betel-chewing as\r
#Text=giving up a favorite food.
#Text=Neither will they be easily persuaded to\r
#Text=indulge in a food of a new description.
#Text=I once showed them the common\r
#Text=British mushroom, which they declared was a poisonous kind.
#Text=To prove\r
#Text=the contrary, I had them several times at table, and found them\r
#Text=precisely similar in appearance and flavor to the well-known, "Agaricus\r
#Text=campestris;" but, notwithstanding this actual proof, the natives would\r
#Text=not be convinced, and, although accustomed to eat a variety of this\r
#Text=tribe, they positively declined this experiment.
#Text=There is an edible\r
#Text=species which they prefer, which, from its appearance, an Englishman\r
#Text=would shun: this is perfectly white, both above and below, and the\r
#Text=upper cuticle cannot be peeled off.
#Text=I have tasted this, but it is very\r
#Text=inferior in flavor to the common mushroom.
#Text=Experiments in these varieties of fungi are highly dangerous, as many\r
#Text=of the most poisonous so closely resemble the edible species that they\r
#Text=can with difficulty be distinguished.
#Text=There is one kind of fungus that\r
#Text=I have met with in the forests which, from its offensive odor and\r
#Text=disgusting appearance, should be something superlatively bad.
#Text=It grows\r
#Text=about four inches high; the top is round, with a fleshy and inflamed\r
#Text=appearance; the stalk is out of all proportion in its thickness, being\r
#Text=about two inches in diameter and of a livid white color; this, when\r
#Text=broken, is full of a transparent gelatinous fluid, which smells like an\r
#Text=egg in the last stage of rottenness.
#Text=This fungus looks like an unhealthy excrescence on the face of Nature,\r
#Text=who, as though ashamed of the disgusting blemish, has thrown a veil\r
#Text=over the defect.
#Text=The most exquisite fabric that can be imagined--a\r
#Text=scarlet veil, like a silken net--falls over this ugly fungus, and,\r
#Text=spreading like a tent at its base, it is there attached to the ground.
#Text=The meshes of this net are about as fine as those of a very delicate\r
#Text=silk purse, and the gaudiness of the color and the size of the fungus\r
#Text=make it a very prominent object, among the surrounding vegetation.
#Text=In\r
#Text=fact, it is a diminutive, though perfect circular tent of net-work, the\r
#Text=stem of the fungus forming the pole in the centre.
#Text=I shall never forget my first introduction to this specimen.
#Text=It was\r
#Text=growing in an open forest, free from any underwood, land it seemed like\r
#Text=a fairy bivouac beneath the mighty trees which overshadowed it.
#Text=Hardly\r
#Text=believing my own eyes at so strange and exquisite a structure, I jumped\r
#Text=off my horse and hastened to secure it.
#Text=But the net-work once raised\r
#Text=was like the uncovering of the veiled prophet of Khorassan, and the\r
#Text=stem, crushing in my fingers, revealed all the disgusting properties of\r
#Text=the plant, and proved the impossibility of removing it entire.
#Text=The\r
#Text=elegance of its exterior only served to conceal its character-like\r
#Text=Madame Mantilini, who, when undressed, "tumbled into ruins."
#Text=There are two varieties of narcotic fungi whose properties are so mild\r
#Text=that they are edible in small quantities.
#Text=One is a bright crimson on\r
#Text=the surface; this is the most powerful, and is seldom used.
#Text=The other\r
#Text=is a white solid puff-ball, with a rough outer skin or rind.
#Text=I have eaten the latter on two occasions, having been assured by the\r
#Text=natives that they were harmless.
#Text=The flavor somewhat resembles a\r
#Text=truffle, but I could not account for the extreme drowsiness that I felt\r
#Text=soon after eating; this wore off in the course of two or three hours.
#Text=On the following day I felt the same effect, but to a still greater\r
#Text=degree as, having convinced myself that they were really eatable, I bad\r
#Text=taken a larger quantity.
#Text=Knowing that the narcotic principle is the\r
#Text=common property of a great variety of fungi, it immediately struck me\r
#Text=that the puff-balls were the cause.
#Text=On questioning the natives, it\r
#Text=appeared that it was this principle that they admired, as it produced a\r
#Text=species of mild intoxication.
#Text=All people, of whatever class or clime, indulge in some narcotic drug\r
#Text=or drink.
#Text=Those of the Cingalese are arrack, tobacco, fungi and the\r
#Text=Indian hemp.
#Text=The use of the latter is, however, not so general among\r
#Text=the Cingalese as the Malabars.
#Text=This drug has a different effect from\r
#Text=opium, as it does not injure the constitution, but simply exhilarates,\r
#Text=and afterward causes a temporary lethargy.
#Text=In appearance it very nearly resembles the common hemp, but it differs\r
#Text=in the seed.
#Text=The leaves and blossoms are dried, and are either smoked\r
#Text=like tobacco, or formed into a paste with various substances and chewed.
#Text=When the plant approaches maturity, a gummy substance exudes from the\r
#Text=leaves; this is gathered by men clothed in dry raw hides, who, by\r
#Text=walking through the plantation, become covered with this gum or glue.
#Text=This is scraped off and carefully preserved, being the very essence of\r
#Text=the plant, and exceedingly powerful in its effects.
#Text=The sensation produced by the properties of this shrub is a wild,\r
#Text=dreamy kind of happiness; the ideas are stimulated to a high degree,\r
#Text=and all that are most pleasurable are exaggerated till the senses at\r
#Text=length sink into a vague and delightful elysium.
#Text=The reaction after this unnatural excitement is very distressing, but\r
#Text=the sufferer is set all right again by some trifling stimulant, such as\r
#Text=a glass of wine or spirits.
#Text=It is supposed, and confidently asserted by some, that the Indian hemp\r
#Text=is the foundation of the Egyptian "hashisch," the effects of which are\r
#Text=precisely similar.
#Text=However harmless the apparent effect of a narcotic drug, common sense\r
#Text=must at once perceive that a repeated intoxication, no matter how it is\r
#Text=produced, must be ultimately hurtful to the system.
#Text=The brain,\r
#Text=accustomed to constant stimulants, at length loses its natural power,\r
#Text=and requires these artificial assistants to enable it to perform its\r
#Text=ordinary functions, in the same manner that the stomach, from similar\r
#Text=treatment, would at length cease to act.
#Text=This being continued, the\r
#Text=brain becomes semi-torpid, until wakened up by a powerful stimulant,\r
#Text=and the nervous system is at length worn out by a succession of\r
#Text=exciting causes and reactions.
#Text=Thus, a hard drinker appears dull and\r
#Text=heavy until under the influence of his secret destroyer when he\r
#Text=brightens up and, perhaps, shines in conversation; but every reaction\r
#Text=requires a stronger amount of stimulant to lessen its effect, until\r
#Text=mind and body at length become involved in the common ruin.
#Text=The seed of the lotus is a narcotic of a mild description, and it is\r
#Text=carefully gathered when ripe and eaten by the natives.
#Text=The lotus is seen in two varieties in Ceylon--the pink and the white.
#Text=The former is the most beautiful, and they are both very common in all\r
#Text=tanks and sluggish streams.
#Text=The leaves are larger than those of the\r
#Text=waterlily, to which they bear a great resemblance, and the blossoms are\r
#Text=full double the size.
#Text=When the latter fade, the petals fall, and the\r
#Text=base of the flower and seed-pod remains in the shape of a circular\r
#Text=piece of honeycomb, full of cells sufficiently large to contain a\r
#Text=hazel-nut.
#Text=This is about the size of the seed, but the shape is more\r
#Text=like an acorn without its cup.
#Text=The flavor is pleasant, being something\r
#Text=like a filbert, but richer and more oily.
#Text=Stramonium (Datura stramonium), which is a powerful narcotic, is a\r
#Text=perfect weed throughout the island, but it is not used by the natives\r
#Text=otherwise than medicinally, and the mass of the people are ignorant of\r
#Text=its qualities, which are only known to the Cingalese doctors.
#Text=I\r
