#Text=recollect some years ago, in Mauritius, where this plant is equally\r
#Text=common, its proprieties were not only fully understood, but made use of\r
#Text=by some of the Chinese emigrants.
#Text=These fellows made cakes of manioc\r
#Text=and poisoned them with stramonium.
#Text=Hot manioc cakes are the common\r
#Text=every-day accompaniment to a French planter's breakfast at Mauritius,\r
#Text=and through the medium of these the Chinese robbed several houses.
#Text=Their plan was simple enough.
#Text=A man with cakes to sell appeared at the house at an early hour, and\r
#Text=these being purchased, he retired until about two hours after breakfast\r
#Text=was concluded.
#Text=By this time the whole family were insensible, and the\r
#Text=thieves robbed the house at their leisure.
#Text=None of these cases\r
#Text=terminated fatally; but, from the instant that I heard of it, I made\r
#Text=every cake-seller who appeared at the door devour one of his own cakes\r
#Text=before I became a purchaser.
#Text=These men, however, were bona fide\r
#Text=cake-merchants, and I did not meet with an exception.
#Text=There are a great variety of valuable medicinal plants in the jungles\r
#Text=of Ceylon, many of which are unknown to any but the native doctors.
#Text=Those most commonly known to us, and which may be seen growing wild by\r
#Text=the roadside, are the nux vomica, ipecacuanha, gamboge, sarsaparilla,\r
#Text=cassia fistula, cardamoms, etc.
#Text=The ipecacuanha is a pretty, delicate plant, which bears a bright\r
#Text=orange-colored cluster of flowers.
#Text=The cassia fistula is a very beautiful tree, growing to the size of an\r
#Text=ash, which it somewhat resembles in foliage.
#Text=The blossom is very\r
#Text=beautiful, being a pendant of golden flowers similar to the laburnum,\r
#Text=but each blossom is about two and a half feet long, and the individual\r
#Text=flowers on the bunch are large in proportion.
#Text=When the tree is in full\r
#Text=flower it is very superb, and equally as singular when its beauty has\r
#Text=faded and the seed-pods are formed.
#Text=These grow to a length of from two\r
#Text=to three feet, and when ripe are perfectly black, round, and about\r
#Text=three-quarters of an inch in diameter.
#Text=The tree has the appearance of\r
#Text=bearing, a prolific crop of ebony rulers, each hanging from the bough\r
#Text=by a short string.
#Text=There is another species of cassia fistula, the foliage of which\r
#Text=assimilates to the mimosa.
#Text=This bears a thicker, but much shorter,\r
#Text=pod, of about a foot in length.
#Text=The properties of both are the same,\r
#Text=being laxative.
#Text=Each seed within the pod is surrounded by a sweet,\r
#Text=black and honey-like substance, which contains the property alluded to.
#Text=The gamboge tree is commonly known in Ceylon as the "ghorka."
#Text=This\r
#Text=grows to the common size of an apple tree, and bears a corrugated and\r
#Text=intensely acid fruit.
#Text=This is dried by the natives and used in\r
#Text=curries.
#Text=The gamboge is the juice of the tree obtained by incisions in\r
#Text=the bark.
#Text=This tree grows in great numbers in the neighborhood of\r
#Text=Colombo, especially among the cinnamon gardens.
#Text=Here, also, the cashew\r
#Text=tree grows to great perfection.
#Text=The bark of the latter is very rich in\r
#Text=tannin, and is used by the natives in the preparation of hides.
#Text=The\r
#Text=fruit is like an apple in appearance, and small, but is highly\r
#Text=astringent.
#Text=The well-known cashew-nut grows like an excrescence from\r
#Text=the end of the apple.
#Text=Many are the varieties and uses of vegetable productions in Ceylon, but\r
#Text=of these none are more singular and interesting than the "sack tree,"\r
#Text=the Riti Gaha of the Cingalese.
#Text=From the bark of this tree an infinite\r
#Text=number of excellent sacks are procured, with very little trouble or\r
#Text=preparation.
#Text=The tree being felled, the branches are cut into logs of\r
#Text=the length required, and sometimes these are soaked in water; but this\r
#Text=is not always necessary.
#Text=The balk is then well beaten with a wooden\r
#Text=mallet, until it is loosened from the wood; it is then stripped off the\r
#Text=log as a stocking is drawn off the leg.
#Text=It is subsequently bleached,\r
#Text=and one end being sewn lip, completes a perfect sack of a thick fibrous\r
#Text=texture, somewhat similar to felt.
#Text=These sacks are in general use among the natives, and are preferred by\r
#Text=them to any other, as their durability is such that they sometimes\r
#Text=descend from father to son.
#Text=By constant use they stretch and increase\r
#Text=their original size nearly one half.
#Text=The texture necessarily becomes\r
#Text=thinner, but the strength does not appear to be materially decreased.
#Text=There are many fibrous barks in Ceylon, some which are so strong that\r
#Text=thin strips require a great amount of strength to break them, but none\r
#Text=of these have yet been reduced to a marketable fibre.
#Text=Several barks\r
#Text=are more or less aromatic; others would be valuable to the tanners;\r
#Text=several are highly esteemed by the natives as most valuable\r
#Text=astringents, but hitherto none have received much notice from\r
#Text=Europeans.
#Text=This may be caused by the general want of success of all\r
#Text=experiments with indigenous produce.
#Text=Although the jungles of Ceylon\r
#Text=produce a long list of articles of much interest, still their value\r
#Text=chiefly lies in their curiosity; they are useful to the native, but\r
#Text=comparatively of little worth to the European.
#Text=In fact, few things will\r
#Text=actually pay for the trouble and expense of collecting and\r
#Text=transporting.
#Text=Throughout the vast forests and jungles of Ceylon,\r
#Text=although the varieties of trees are endless, there is not one valuable\r
#Text=gum known to exist.
#Text=There is a great variety of coarse, unmarketable\r
#Text=productions, about equal to the gum of the cherry tree, etc., but there\r
#Text=is no such thing as a high-priced gum in the island.
#Text=The export of dammer is a mere trifle--four tons in 1852, twelve tons\r
#Text=in 1853.
#Text=This is a coarse and comparatively valueless commodity.
#Text=No\r
#Text=other tree but the doom tree produces any gum worth collecting; this\r
#Text=species of rosin exudes in large quantities from an incision in the\r
#Text=bark, but the amount of exports shows its insignificance.
#Text=It is a fair\r
#Text=sample of Ceylon productions; nothing that is uncultivated is of much\r
#Text=pecuniary value.
#Text=Fish and fruit are the most enjoyable articles of food in a tropical\r
#Text=country, and in the former Ceylon is rich.
#Text=The seir fish is little\r
#Text=inferior to salmon, and were the flesh a similar color, it might\r
#Text=sometimes form a substitute.
#Text=Soles and whiting remind us of Old\r
#Text=England, but a host of bright red, blue, green, yellow, and\r
#Text=extraordinary-looking creatures in the same net dispel all ideas of\r
#Text=English fishing.
#Text=Oysters there are likewise in Ceylon; but here, alas I there is a sad\r
#Text=falling off in the comparison with our well-remembered "native."
#Text=Instead of the neat little shell of the English oyster, the Ceylon\r
#Text=species is a shapeless, twisted, knotty, rocky-looking creature, such\r
#Text=as a legitimate oyster would be in a fit of spasms or convulsions.
#Text=In\r
#Text=fact, there is no vestige of the true breed about it, and the want of\r
#Text=flavor equals its miserable exterior.
#Text=There are few positions more tantalizing to a hungry man than that of\r
#Text=being surrounded b oysters without a knife.
#Text=It is an obstinate and\r
#Text=perverse wretch that will not accommodate itself to man's appetite, and\r
#Text=it requires a forcible attack to vanquish it; so that every oyster\r
#Text=eaten is an individual murder, in which the cold steel has been plunged\r
#Text=into its vitals, and the animal finds itself swallowed before it as\r
#Text=quite made up its mind that it has been opened.
#Text=But take away the\r
#Text=knife, and see how vain is the attempt to force the stronghold.
#Text=How\r
#Text=utterly useless is the oyster!
#Text=You may turn it over and over, and look\r
#Text=for a weak place, but there is no admittance; you may knock it with a\r
#Text=stone, but the knock will be unanswered.
#Text=How would you open such a\r
#Text=creature without a knife?
#Text=Palliser and I having eaten carefully, were just beginning to feel\r
#Text=uncomfortable, when up jumped Benton, holding his throat with both\r
#Text=hands, crying, "My throat's full of pins.
#Text=poisoned, no doubt of it," said Palliser, in his turn.
#Text="I am choking\r
#Text=likewise."
#Text="So am I."
#Text=There we were all three, with our throats in an\r
#Text=extraordinary state of sudden contraction and inflammation, with a\r
#Text=burning and pricking sensation, in addition to a feeling of swelling\r
#Text=and stoppage of the windpipe.
#Text=Having nothing but brandy at hand, we\r
#Text=dosed largely instanter, and in the course of ten minutes we found\r
#Text=relief; but Benton, having, eaten his large yam, was the last to\r
#Text=recover.
#Text=There must have been highly poisonous qualities in this root, as the\r
#Text=quantity eaten was nothing in proportion to the effects produced.
#Text=It\r
#Text=is well known that many roots are poisonous when raw (especially the\r
#Text=manioc), which become harmless when cooked, as the noxious properties\r
#Text=consist of a very volatile oil, which is thrown off during the process\r
#Text=of boiling.
#Text=These wild yams must necessarily be still worse in their\r
#Text=raw state; and it struck me, after their effects became known, that I\r
#Text=had never seen them grubbed up by the wild hogs; this neglect being a\r
#Text=sure proof of their unfitness for food.
